one_line_takeaway: CVE-Bench is a reproducible, auto-graded sandbox of 40 real critical web CVEs showing that current LLM agents can autonomously exploit up to ~13% of them (one-day) / ~10% (zero-day), with insufficient exploration the main bottleneck.
key_finding: State-of-the-art agents can exploit a meaningful minority of real critical CVEs but remain far from proficient. Under the one-day setting the best agent reaches up to 13% success@5; under zero-day up to 10% success@5. T-Agent (improved with sqlmap) is the strongest, largely because it can execute a full SQL-injection workflow, and among its successful zero-day exploits 68% were database access. Cy-Agent is weakest (only 2.5% success@5 one-day) because its focused CTF-style loop does not suit the extensive exploration these tasks demand. The dominant failure mode across all agents is insufficient exploration (up to 80% of T-Agent zero-day runs). AutoGPT's self-criticism helps it fix technical errors and it achieved the highest success@5, even occasionally doing better on zero-day than one-day by finding an easier alternative vulnerability than the one described.
core_contribution: CVE-Bench, presented as the first real-world cybersecurity benchmark for evaluating whether LLM agents can autonomously exploit web-application vulnerabilities. The authors' own contribution is (1) a curated set of 40 critical-severity CVEs (CVSS 3.x >= 9.0) drawn from the National Vulnerability Database for open-source web applications, each containerized into a reproducible 'target container' sandbox with a reference exploit as proof-of-concept; (2) a standardization of unpredictable attacks into eight measurable 'standard attacks' with an automatic grading server co-hosted with each app; and (3) simulation of two vulnerability-lifecycle stages, zero-day (task description only) and one-day (NVD vulnerability description provided as a hint). This is distinct from the cited prior benchmarks it compares against (Cybench / Zhang et al. 2024a, and Fang et al. 2024a/c), which are baselines/related work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Autonomous LLM agents used offensively to exploit real-world, critical-severity web-application vulnerabilities without human operators. The benchmark models an attacker (the agent) that is given a web endpoint and instructed to achieve any one of eight attack objectives (DoS, file access, file creation, database modification, database access, unauthorized administrator login, privilege escalation, outbound service/SSRF). Two knowledge levels are modeled: zero-day (agent has only the task/endpoint and must independently discover and exploit the flaw) and one-day (agent additionally receives the high-level NVD vulnerability description). Out-of-scope actions are disallowed (attacking the evaluator, brute-forcing passwords; random strings are used as admin/DB passwords to discourage brute force). Agents are prompted as white-hat hackers with owner permission so none refuse.
limitations: Authors state: the benchmark cannot evaluate attacks outside the eight predefined standard attacks, risking false negatives; it covers only 40 web-related CVEs from a narrow publication window (May 1 - June 14, 2024). Additional reviewer-noted constraints grounded in the text: only open-source, platform-independent, single-vulnerability (no chaining) CVEs were included (60 initially met criteria, only 40 were reproducible); only 24 of the 40 tasks have login interfaces and separate databases so not all eight attacks apply to every task; main experiments use a single model (gpt-4o-2024-11-20) with a 30-iteration cap; framing agents as authorized white-hat hackers removes refusal behavior, which may inflate attack rates versus safety-aligned deployments.

datasets_used: CVE-Bench: 40 critical-severity CVEs (CVSS 3.1 base score >= 9.0) from the National Vulnerability Database, web applications published between May 1 and June 14, 2024 (60 met selection criteria, 40 successfully reproduced)

Application-type distribution: Content management 12, AI/ML 7, Business management 6, Operational monitoring 4, Web infrastructure 3, Library/package 3, E-commerce 2, Computing management 1, Mail server 1, Web portal 1
24 of the 40 CVEs have login interfaces and use separate databases (so all eight standard attacks are graded)
quantitative_results: Best one-day success@5: up to 13% (12.5% cited in text; T-Agent)
T-Agent successful zero-day exploits that were database access: 68% (30% under one-day); AutoGPT database access: 0% in both settings
Dominant failure mode 'Insufficient Exploration' frequency: T-Agent 80% (zero-day) / 55% (one-day); AutoGPT 72.5% / 45%; Cy-Agent 67.5% / 37.5%
Manual exploit reproduction cost: 5-24 person-hours per CVE
Per-task monetary cost: Cy-Agent $0.6 (zero-day)/$0.7 (one-day); T-Agent $1.7/$1.7; AutoGPT $0.8/$1.0; total benchmark run < $100
Per-task input/output tokens (avg of 5 reps): Cy-Agent ~142k in / ~28-30k out; T-Agent ~627-643k in / ~8k out; AutoGPT ~284-341k in / ~12k out
Model gpt-4o-2024-11-20, 30 iterations/task, 5 repetitions per setting
baselines_compared: Cy-Agent (Cybench agent, Zhang et al. 2024a) - act/execute/update ReAct-style loop
T-Agent (Teams of LLM agents, Fang et al. 2024c) - hierarchical supervisor + specialized hacker agents, improved here with sqlmap for SQL injection
AutoGPT (Richards 2023) - general autonomous agent with plan/reason/self-criticize loop
Benchmark-level comparison in Table 1 against Cybench (40 vulns, not real-world/critical) and Fang et al. 2024a/c (25 vulns, real-world but limited diversity/one attack type per CVE)
